1:
"Let us therefore fear, lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest, any of you should seem to come short of it." The witnesses note the godly fear lest any come short of the promised rest. Gill notes this fear is not a fear of wrath and damnation, nor a fear of diffidence and distrust of God's power and goodness, but a cautious fear, a godly jealousy, a careful circumspection and watchfulness. The witnesses note Paul charging that we fear, lest, a promise of entering into his rest being left us, any should seem to come short of it; the holy fear that guards against falling short of God's rest — that Paul draws the practical conclusion from Israel's fall: "let us therefore fear" — not with a slavish dread, but with a reverent, watchful caution — "lest, a promise being left us of entering into his rest" (for the gospel rest is still held out, the promise yet standing open) "any of you should seem to come short of it"; so that, since the rest remains to be entered and the promise is still ours, the great danger is not God's failing but our own falling short through unbelief, and against this a holy, vigilant fear is the proper safeguard; lest any of you should seem to come short of it. The witnesses note let us therefore fear. The witnesses note lest any of you should seem to come short of it.

The witnesses note the godly fear lest any come short of the promised rest — not slavish dread but a reverent, watchful caution, since the promise of rest still stands open.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy fear that guards against falling short. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since the rest still remains, a reverent, watchful fear is the safeguard against coming short through unbelief. The witnesses note the fear: "let us therefore fear" — Gill notes a cautious, godly fear, not slavish dread; the fear; the cautious; the godly; the not slavish; the watchful. The expositors observe the not damnation: not a fear of wrath or distrust of God; the not damnation; the not distrust; the not slavish; the godly; the reverent. The witnesses press the promise left: "a promise being left us of entering into his rest" — the gospel rest is still held out; the promise left; the the rest; the held out; the still standing; the open. The expositors draw the lesson on the come short: "lest any of you should seem to come short of it" — lest any fall short; the come short; the fall short; the any; the the danger; the miss. The witnesses observe the our falling: the danger is our own falling short, not God's failing; the our falling; the not God's failing; the through unbelief; the our own; the short. The expositors note the safeguard: holy, vigilant fear is the safeguard; the safeguard; the vigilant fear; the holy; the watchful; the guards. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us therefore fear, the lest any of you should seem to come short of it, and the godly fear. The verse thus records the holy fear that guards against falling short — teaching that, since the rest still remains, a reverent fear is the safeguard against coming short through unbelief.
2:
"For unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them: but the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard it." The witnesses note that the word profits only when mixed with faith. Gill notes the gospel is the good news of salvation by Christ; it is said to be preached when men preach not themselves, nor read lectures of morality, nor mix law and gospel, nor make salvation to be by works. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the gospel was preached to us as well as to them, but the word did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in the hearers; the word unprofitable without faith mixing with it — that Paul presses the parallel between Israel and the present hearers: "unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them" (a glad message of rest was held out to the wilderness generation, as it is to us) — "but the word preached did not profit them" — it brought them no benefit, did them no saving good — and Paul gives the reason: "not being mixed with faith in them that heard it"; so that the bare hearing of the word avails nothing unless it be received and blended with faith, faith being the necessary thing that makes the word profitable to the soul, its absence the cause of all Israel's loss; the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith. The witnesses note unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them. The witnesses note the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard it.

The witnesses note the word unprofitable without faith — the gospel preached to Israel brought no benefit, not being mixed with faith; the bare hearing avails nothing without believing.

So the witnesses make the verse the word profitable only when mixed with faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that the bare hearing of the word avails nothing unless it is received and blended with faith. The witnesses note the gospel preached: "unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them" — Gill notes a glad message of rest held out to both; the gospel preached; the to us; the to them; the the message; the rest. The expositors observe the did not profit: "but the word preached did not profit them" — it brought them no benefit; the did not profit; the no benefit; the no good; the them; the unprofitable. The witnesses press the not mixed: "not being mixed with faith" — not blended with faith; the not mixed; the not blended; the with faith; the the reason; the absent. The expositors draw the lesson on the in them that heard: "in them that heard it" — the hearers lacked faith; the in them that heard; the the hearers; the lacked faith; the unbelief; the heard. The witnesses observe the bare hearing: the bare hearing avails nothing without faith; the bare hearing; the avails nothing; the without faith; the alone; the unprofitable. The expositors note the faith necessary: faith is the necessary thing that makes the word profitable; the faith necessary; the makes profitable; the the word; the the soul; the necessary. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto us was the gospel preached as well as unto them, the the word preached did not profit them not being mixed with faith in them that heard it, and the word without faith. The verse thus records the word profitable only when mixed with faith — teaching that the bare hearing of the word avails nothing unless received and blended with faith.
3:
"For we which have believed do enter into rest, as he said, As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest: although the works were finished from the foundation of the world." The witnesses note that believers do enter the rest from which unbelievers were barred. Gill notes this rest is not eternal rest only, which all believers shall enjoy, but spiritual rest in Christ under the gospel dispensation, into which believers now enter. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we which have believed do enter into rest, as God said the unbelievers should not, though the works were finished from the foundation of the world; the rest entered by faith, denied to unbelief — that Paul confirms the necessity of faith from the contrast: "we which have believed do enter into rest" — faith is the very door into the rest, so that believers now enter the spiritual rest in Christ — whereas, as the oath showed, the unbelieving were sworn out: "As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter into my rest" (a Hebrew form meaning "they shall not enter") — and Paul adds a note that prepares his next argument: "although the works were finished from the foundation of the world," that is, God's own rest had existed ever since creation, yet that primal rest was not the whole of what was meant; we which have believed do enter into rest. The witnesses note we which have believed do enter into rest. The witnesses note although the works were finished from the foundation of the world.

The witnesses note that believers enter the rest denied to unbelief — faith the very door into the rest in Christ; God's own rest had existed since creation, yet more was meant.

So the witnesses make the verse the rest entered by faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith is the door into the rest, denied to the unbelieving, though God's rest had existed since creation. The witnesses note the believed enter: "we which have believed do enter into rest" — Gill notes faith is the door into the rest in Christ; the believed enter; the faith; the the door; the the rest; the in Christ. The expositors observe the spiritual rest: this is spiritual rest in Christ under the gospel; the spiritual rest; the in Christ; the the gospel; the now; the the rest. The witnesses press the sworn out: "As I have sworn in my wrath, if they shall enter" — the unbelieving were sworn out; the sworn out; the the oath; the the unbelieving; the not enter; the excluded. The expositors draw the lesson on the Hebrew form: "if they shall enter" is a Hebrew form for "they shall not enter"; the Hebrew form; the not enter; the the idiom; the the oath; the excluded. The witnesses observe the works finished: "although the works were finished from the foundation of the world" — God's rest existed since creation; the works finished; the the foundation; the creation; the God's rest; the since. The expositors note the more was meant: yet that primal rest was not the whole meant; the more was meant; the the primal rest; the not the whole; the another; the prepared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we which have believed do enter into rest, the although the works were finished from the foundation of the world, and the rest entered by faith. The verse thus records the rest entered by faith — teaching that faith is the door into the rest, denied to the unbelieving.
4:
"For he spake in a certain place of the seventh day on this wise, And God did rest the seventh day from all his works." The witnesses note the rest of God on the seventh day of creation. Barnes notes that at the close of the work of creation God rested — the work was done; that was the rest of God, who was happy in the contemplation of his own works, and instituted the sabbath. The witnesses note Paul citing that God rested the seventh day from all his works; the original rest of God after creation as the first sense of rest — that Paul, building his argument that a rest "remains," appeals first to the original rest: "he spake in a certain place of the seventh day" (in Genesis) "And God did rest the seventh day from all his works" — the rest of God himself, who, having finished the work of creation, ceased and rested on the seventh day, and hallowed it; so that there is a "rest of God" spoken of from the very beginning, the pattern and root of all the rest that follows, into which God invites his people to enter; And God did rest the seventh day from all his works. The witnesses note he spake in a certain place of the seventh day. The witnesses note And God did rest the seventh day from all his works.

The witnesses note the original rest of God after creation — God resting the seventh day from all his works; the pattern and root of all the rest that follows.

So the witnesses make the verse the original rest of God after creation. The lesson the expositors draw is that there is a "rest of God" from the beginning, the pattern of all the rest into which God invites his people. The witnesses note the spake: "he spake in a certain place of the seventh day" — Paul appeals to Genesis; the spake; the a certain place; the the seventh day; the Genesis; the cited. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes God rested, happy in his works, instituting the sabbath; the rested; the his works; the happy; the the sabbath; the instituted. The witnesses press the God did rest: "And God did rest the seventh day" — God himself rested; the God did rest; the himself; the rested; the the seventh day; the ceased. The expositors draw the lesson on the from all his works: "from all his works" — having finished creation, he ceased; the from all his works; the finished; the creation; the ceased; the rested. The witnesses observe the the rest of God: there is a "rest of God" from the beginning; the the rest of God; the from the beginning; the spoken of; the the pattern; the the root. The expositors note the invites his people: the pattern of the rest God invites his people to enter; the invites his people; the the pattern; the enter; the his people; the the rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he spake in a certain place of the seventh day, the And God did rest the seventh day from all his works, and the original rest of God. The verse thus records the original rest of God after creation — teaching that there is a "rest of God" from the beginning, the pattern of all the rest into which God invites his people.
5:
"And in this place again, If they shall enter into my rest." The witnesses note the second mention of rest, distinct from the seventh-day rest. Gill notes that in this place (Psalm 95) God speaks again of another rest, distinct from that on the seventh day; and it is this latter, not the former, which believers under the gospel dispensation enter into. The witnesses note Paul citing again, "If they shall enter into my rest"; the second rest spoken of in the Psalm, proving a further rest remains — that Paul sets the two Scriptures side by side: the seventh-day rest of Genesis (v.4) and now, "in this place again" (in the Psalm written long after), the rest still spoken of as future and unentered — "If they shall enter into my rest"; so that, since God in David's day still speaks of "my rest" as a thing not yet entered, the original creation-rest cannot be the whole of what is meant — there is a further, distinct rest yet held out, which the argument will press home, the rest into which gospel believers enter; If they shall enter into my rest. The witnesses note and in this place again. The witnesses note If they shall enter into my rest.

The witnesses note the second mention of rest in the Psalm — distinct from the seventh-day rest, still spoken of as future, proving a further rest yet remains to be entered.

So the witnesses make the verse the second, distinct rest still held out. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since God in the Psalm still speaks of "my rest" as unentered, a further rest remains beyond the creation-rest. The witnesses note the in this place again: "and in this place again" — Gill notes the Psalm speaks of another rest; the in this place again; the the Psalm; the another rest; the distinct; the later. The expositors observe the distinct from seventh day: distinct from the seventh-day rest; the distinct; the from the seventh day; the another; the different; the later. The witnesses press the my rest: "If they shall enter into my rest" — God still speaks of "my rest" as future; the my rest; the still future; the unentered; the spoken of; the held out. The expositors draw the lesson on the not yet entered: in David's day still a thing not yet entered; the not yet entered; the David's day; the future; the unentered; the held out. The witnesses observe the not the whole: the creation-rest cannot be the whole meant; the not the whole; the the creation-rest; the not all; the another; the further. The expositors note the further rest: there is a further, distinct rest held out; the further rest; the distinct; the held out; the gospel; the remains. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and in this place again, the If they shall enter into my rest, and the second rest. The verse thus records the second, distinct rest still held out — teaching that, since God in the Psalm still speaks of "my rest" as unentered, a further rest remains beyond the creation-rest.
6:
"Seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein, and they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief:" The witnesses note that the rest must be entered by some, the first hearers having failed through unbelief. Barnes notes that since there is a rest spoken of in the Scriptures, implying it is to be enjoyed by some, and since they to whom it was first promised did not inherit it, it follows that others are to partake of it. The witnesses note Paul reasoning that, since it remains that some must enter the rest, and the first hearers entered not because of unbelief, the rest is still open; the rest left open for others since the first hearers forfeited it — that Paul draws the inference from the two Scriptures: "it remaineth that some must enter therein" (since God's promise of rest cannot fail, some must certainly enter and enjoy it) — but "they to whom it was first preached" (the wilderness generation) "entered not in because of unbelief" (forfeited it by their unbelief) — so that, the first invited having fallen short, the rest stands open still, waiting to be entered by others, the promise not exhausted but reaching forward to a people who will believe; seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein. The witnesses note it remaineth that some must enter therein. The witnesses note they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief.

The witnesses note the rest left open for others — since God's promise cannot fail, some must enter, and the first hearers having forfeited it by unbelief, the rest still stands open.

So the witnesses make the verse the rest left open since the first hearers forfeited it. The lesson the expositors draw is that, the first invited having fallen short, the rest stands open still, to be entered by a believing people. The witnesses note the remaineth: "it remaineth that some must enter therein" — Barnes notes God's promise implies some must enjoy it; the remaineth; the some must enter; the the promise; the cannot fail; the certainly. The expositors observe the cannot fail: God's promise of rest cannot fail; the cannot fail; the the promise; the some; the must enter; the sure. The witnesses press the first preached: "they to whom it was first preached" — the wilderness generation; the first preached; the the generation; the the first hearers; the the wilderness; the invited. The expositors draw the lesson on the entered not: "entered not in because of unbelief" — they forfeited it by unbelief; the entered not; the forfeited; the unbelief; the fell short; the lost. The witnesses observe the open still: the rest stands open still for others; the open still; the for others; the waiting; the the rest; the held out. The expositors note the not exhausted: the promise not exhausted but reaching forward; the not exhausted; the reaching forward; the a believing people; the the promise; the open. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the it remaineth that some must enter therein, the they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of unbelief, and the rest left open. The verse thus records the rest left open since the first hearers forfeited it — teaching that, the first invited having fallen short, the rest stands open still, to be entered by a believing people.
7:
"Again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day, after so long a time; as it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts." The witnesses note that God fixes a new "today" long after, in David. Gill notes that since the seventh day was a rest God entered, not man, and the land of Canaan a typical rest the unbelieving Israelites did not enter, there must yet be persons and a time for entering. The witnesses note Paul observing that God again limits a certain day, "Today," in David, long after, saying, "Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts"; the new day of opportunity fixed long after Canaan was given — that Paul presses the proof one step further: God "limiteth" (appoints, marks out) "a certain day, saying in David, To day" — and that "after so long a time," that is, long after the wilderness and long after Canaan was possessed, in the days of David, God still names a "Today" of opportunity to hear his voice; so that the existence of this fresh "Today" in David's Psalm proves that the true rest was not the seventh-day rest, nor exhausted by the entry into Canaan, but still stood open, a present day of grace yet calling men to enter; To day, after so long a time. The witnesses note again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day. The witnesses note To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts.

The witnesses note God fixing a fresh "Today" in David — long after Canaan was given — proving the true rest was not exhausted by Canaan but still stood open as a present day of grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the new day of opportunity fixed long after Canaan. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's fresh "Today" in David proves the true rest was not exhausted by Canaan but stood open still. The witnesses note the limiteth a day: "he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, To day" — God appoints a new "Today" in David; the limiteth a day; the appoints; the marks out; the in David; the Today. The expositors observe the after so long: "after so long a time" — long after the wilderness and Canaan; the after so long; the long after; the the wilderness; the Canaan; the later. The witnesses press the still names today: God still names a "Today" of opportunity; the still names today; the Today; the opportunity; the to hear; the present. The expositors draw the lesson on Gill's note: Gill notes there must yet be persons and a time for entering; the Gill's note; the persons; the a time; the entering; the remains. The witnesses observe the not exhausted by Canaan: the rest was not exhausted by the entry into Canaan; the not exhausted; the by Canaan; the still open; the the rest; the remains. The expositors note the present day of grace: a present day of grace yet calling men to enter; the present day of grace; the calling; the to enter; the open; the Today. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the again he limiteth a certain day saying in David To day, the To day if ye will hear his voice harden not your hearts, and the new day of opportunity. The verse thus records the new day of opportunity fixed long after Canaan — teaching that God's fresh "Today" in David proves the true rest stood open still.
8:
"For if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day." The witnesses note that Joshua's rest in Canaan was not the final rest. Gill notes that by "Jesus" is meant Joshua, for Hosheah, Joshua, and Jesus are the same name; Joshua is called Jesus by the Septuagint and by Josephus and Philo. The witnesses note Paul reasoning that if Joshua had given them rest, God would not afterward have spoken of another day; the proof that Canaan-rest under Joshua was not the true and final rest — that Paul completes the proof: "if Jesus" — that is, Joshua, who led Israel into Canaan — "had given them rest" (had brought them into the full and final rest of God by settling them in the land), "then would he not afterward have spoken of another day"; but God did, long afterward in David, speak of yet "another day" of rest — and this proves that the rest Joshua gave (the earthly Canaan) was not the true and final rest, but only a type and shadow of a better, spiritual, and eternal rest still to come, the rest that remains for the people of God; for if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day. The witnesses note for if Jesus had given them rest. The witnesses note then would he not afterward have spoken of another day.

The witnesses note that Joshua's Canaan-rest was not the final rest — for God later spoke of "another day"; the earthly Canaan but a type of a better, spiritual, eternal rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the proof that Canaan-rest was not final. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since God later spoke of another day, Joshua's Canaan-rest was only a type of a better rest still to come. The witnesses note the Jesus is Joshua: "if Jesus had given them rest" — Gill notes "Jesus" here means Joshua; the Jesus is Joshua; the Joshua; the the same name; the led Israel; the Canaan. The expositors observe the given them rest: had Joshua brought them into the full rest; the given them rest; the the full rest; the the land; the settled; the Canaan. The witnesses press the another day: "then would he not afterward have spoken of another day" — but God spoke of another day; the another day; the afterward; the God spoke; the in David; the later. The expositors draw the lesson on the not final: this proves Canaan-rest was not the final rest; the not final; the the Canaan-rest; the not the true rest; the only a type; the shadow. The witnesses observe the type and shadow: the earthly Canaan was a type of a better rest; the type and shadow; the the earthly Canaan; the a type; the better; the spiritual. The expositors note the remains: a spiritual, eternal rest still to come remains; the remains; the spiritual; the eternal; the to come; the the people of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for if Jesus had given them rest, the then would he not afterward have spoken of another day, and the Canaan-rest not final. The verse thus records the proof that Canaan-rest was not final — teaching that, since God later spoke of another day, Joshua's Canaan-rest was only a type of a better rest still to come.
9:
"There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God." The witnesses note the conclusion — a rest remains for God's people. Barnes notes this is the conclusion to which the apostle comes — the result of the whole reasoning, that there is a rest, distinct from the rest of creation and from the rest of Canaan, reserved for the people of God. The witnesses note Paul concluding that there remains a rest to the people of God; the great conclusion that a sabbath-rest remains for believers — that Paul gathers the whole argument into its triumphant conclusion: "there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God" — since the seventh-day rest was God's own, since Canaan under Joshua was not the final rest, and since God still spoke of a "Today" in David, it follows that a true rest yet "remaineth," reserved and held in store, "to the people of God" (to believers, the true Israel); the witnesses note that the word here is properly a "sabbath-rest" (a sabbatism), a settled, sabbath-like rest of the soul in God, begun in grace and consummated in glory, which is the abiding inheritance of all who believe; there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. The witnesses note there remaineth therefore a rest. The witnesses note to the people of God.

The witnesses note the great conclusion — a rest remains for God's people; a sabbath-rest of the soul in God, begun in grace and consummated in glory, the inheritance of all who believe.

So the witnesses make the verse the conclusion that a rest remains for believers. The lesson the expositors draw is that a true sabbath-rest yet remains, reserved for the people of God who believe. The witnesses note the remaineth: "there remaineth therefore a rest" — Barnes notes the conclusion of the whole reasoning; the remaineth; the the conclusion; the the reasoning; the a rest; the reserved. The expositors observe the distinct: distinct from the creation-rest and Canaan; the distinct; the the creation-rest; the Canaan; the another; the the rest. The witnesses press the people of God: "to the people of God" — to believers, the true Israel; the people of God; the believers; the the true Israel; the held in store; the theirs. The expositors draw the lesson on the sabbath-rest: the word is a "sabbath-rest" (a sabbatism); the sabbath-rest; the sabbatism; the settled; the sabbath-like; the the soul. The witnesses observe the begun in grace: a rest begun in grace, consummated in glory; the begun in grace; the in grace; the consummated; the in glory; the the soul. The expositors note the inheritance: the abiding inheritance of all who believe; the the inheritance; the abiding; the all who believe; the theirs; the the rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there remaineth therefore a rest, the to the people of God, and the rest that remains. The verse thus records the conclusion that a rest remains for believers — teaching that a true sabbath-rest yet remains, reserved for the people of God who believe.
10:
"For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his." The witnesses note the nature of the rest — ceasing from one's own works. Gill notes this is to be understood not of believers entering the gospel or eternal rest, but of the Lord Jesus Christ; for a single person is spoken of, and the rest is "his own," which cannot be said of any other. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he that is entered into his rest has ceased from his own works, as God did from his; the rest as a ceasing from one's works, after the pattern of God — that Paul describes the character of this rest by analogy with God's: "he that is entered into his rest" — the witnesses note Gill takes this chiefly of Christ, who, having finished his work of redemption, entered his own rest, as God ceased from creation — yet the principle applies to all who enter: to enter the rest is to have "ceased from his own works, as God did from his," that is, to rest from the toil and labour of self-justifying works, leaning wholly on the finished work of another, even as God rested from creation having finished it; so that gospel rest is a sabbath of the soul, ceasing from one's own works to rest in God's; he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his. The witnesses note he that is entered into his rest. The witnesses note he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his.

The witnesses note the rest as a ceasing from one's own works, after God's pattern — resting from the toil of self-justifying works to lean wholly on the finished work of another.

So the witnesses make the verse the rest as ceasing from one's works. The lesson the expositors draw is that to enter the rest is to cease from self-justifying works, as God ceased from creation. The witnesses note the entered his rest: "he that is entered into his rest" — Gill takes this chiefly of Christ entering his own rest; the entered his rest; the chiefly Christ; the his own rest; the finished; the redemption. The expositors observe the the principle: the principle applies to all who enter; the the principle; the all who enter; the applies; the the rest; the believers. The witnesses press the ceased from works: "he also hath ceased from his own works" — ceased from the toil of self-justifying works; the ceased from works; the the toil; the self-justifying; the rest; the leaning. The expositors draw the lesson on the as God did: "as God did from his" — as God rested from creation, having finished it; the as God did; the rested; the from creation; the finished; the the pattern. The witnesses observe the finished work of another: leaning wholly on the finished work of another; the finished work of another; the leaning; the wholly; the not own; the Christ's. The expositors note the sabbath of the soul: gospel rest is a sabbath of the soul; the sabbath of the soul; the gospel rest; the ceasing; the in God; the rest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that is entered into his rest, the he also hath ceased from his own works as God did from his, and the rest as ceasing. The verse thus records the rest as ceasing from one's works — teaching that to enter the rest is to cease from self-justifying works, as God ceased from creation.
11:
"Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief." The witnesses note the charge to labour to enter the rest, lest any fall. Chrysostom notes faith is great and brings salvation, and without it none can be saved, yet it suffices not of itself, but there is need of a right conversation also; so Paul exhorts those already counted worthy of the mysteries to labour. The witnesses note Paul charging that we labour to enter that rest, lest any fall after the same example of unbelief; the diligent striving to enter the rest, warned by Israel's example — that Paul draws the practical exhortation from the whole argument: since a rest remains, "let us labour therefore to enter into that rest" — give all diligence, strive earnestly (a paradox: to labour to enter a rest), pressing on by faith and obedience to lay hold of it — "lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief," that is, lest any follow the wilderness generation in their fatal unbelief and so, like them, fall short and perish; so that the way into the rest is by diligent, persevering faith, the warning of Israel's fall ever before us; let us labour therefore to enter into that rest. The witnesses note let us labour therefore to enter into that rest. The witnesses note lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief.

The witnesses note the charge to labour to enter the rest — striving earnestly by faith — lest any fall after Israel's example of unbelief and so come short and perish.

So the witnesses make the verse the diligent striving to enter the rest. The lesson the expositors draw is that we are to labour by faith to enter the rest, lest we fall after Israel's example of unbelief. The witnesses note the labour: "let us labour therefore to enter into that rest" — give all diligence, strive earnestly; the labour; the diligence; the strive; the earnestly; the press on. The expositors observe the paradox: a paradox — to labour to enter a rest; the paradox; the labour; the a rest; the strive; the press. The witnesses press Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes faith saves, yet needs a right conversation too; the Chrysostom; the faith saves; the right conversation; the labour; the both. The expositors draw the lesson on the by faith: pressing on by faith and obedience; the by faith; the obedience; the press on; the lay hold; the diligent. The witnesses observe the lest fall: "lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief" — lest any follow Israel's unbelief; the lest fall; the Israel's example; the unbelief; the come short; the perish. The expositors note the warning before us: the warning of Israel's fall ever before us; the warning before us; the Israel's fall; the the warning; the example; the heed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, the lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief, and the striving to enter. The verse thus records the diligent striving to enter the rest — teaching that we are to labour by faith to enter, lest we fall after Israel's example of unbelief.
12:
"For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart." The witnesses note the living, piercing power of the word of God. Gill notes this is to be understood of Christ, the essential Word of God, for "the Word of God" was a known name of the Messiah among the Jews, and so the apostle uses it when writing to them. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the word of God is quick, powerful, sharper than a twoedged sword, piercing to dividing soul and spirit, and discerning the thoughts of the heart; the searching, dividing power of the word that lays the heart bare — that Paul enforces the warning by the searching power before which all stands open: "the word of God is quick" (living) "and powerful" (active and mighty), "sharper than any twoedged sword" — and it pierces deeper than any blade, "to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow," reaching the innermost being — and it "is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart," laying bare the secret motions of the soul; the witnesses note the debate whether "the word of God" here is the personal Word, Christ himself (so Gill), or the spoken/written word of God, both agreeing it searches and judges the heart; the word of God... is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart. The witnesses note the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword. The witnesses note a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.

The witnesses note the living, piercing word of God — sharper than any sword, dividing soul and spirit, discerning the thoughts of the heart; debated as Christ or the spoken word.

So the witnesses make the verse the searching power of the word that lays the heart bare. The lesson the expositors draw is that the word of God is living and piercing, dividing the innermost being and discerning the heart's thoughts. The witnesses note the quick and powerful: "the word of God is quick, and powerful" — living, active, and mighty; the quick and powerful; the living; the active; the mighty; the the word. The expositors present the debate: the witnesses note the debate — whether "the word of God" is the personal Word, Christ himself (Gill), or the spoken and written word; both agree it searches the heart. The witnesses press the sharper: "sharper than any twoedged sword" — sharper than any blade; the sharper; the twoedged sword; the any blade; the cuts; the pierces. The expositors draw the lesson on the dividing: "piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow" — reaching the innermost being; the dividing; the soul and spirit; the joints and marrow; the innermost; the pierces. The witnesses observe the discerner: "a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart" — laying bare the heart's secret motions; the discerner; the thoughts; the intents; the the heart; the bare. The expositors note the searches and judges: it searches and judges the heart; the searches and judges; the the heart; the bare; the secret; the laid open. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the word of God is quick and powerful and sharper than any twoedged sword, the a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart, and the searching word. The verse thus records the searching power of the word — teaching that the word of God is living and piercing, dividing the innermost being and discerning the heart's thoughts.
13:
"Neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight: but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do." The witnesses note that all creatures are open and naked before God's sight. Gill notes Christ is the Lord God omniscient; there is no creature, rational or irrational, animate or inanimate, but is known to him and seen by him, for all creatures are made and upheld by him, and he is omnipresent. The witnesses note Paul declaring that no creature is hidden from his sight, but all things are naked and opened to the eyes of him with whom we have to do; the omniscient gaze before which nothing is hidden — that Paul presses the warning home with the all-seeing knowledge of God (or of Christ the Word): "neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight" (nothing in all creation is hidden from him) — "but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do," that is, every thing lies bare, exposed, laid open (as a sacrifice flayed and split) before the eyes of the One to whom we must give account; so that, since the Judge sees every secret of the heart, the warning against a hidden unbelief is the more solemn — there is no hiding from him with whom we have to do; all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do. The witnesses note neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight. The witnesses note all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do.

The witnesses note the omniscient gaze before which nothing is hidden — all things naked and opened to the eyes of him with whom we have to do, the Judge who sees every secret.

So the witnesses make the verse the all-seeing knowledge before which nothing is hidden. The lesson the expositors draw is that nothing in creation is hidden from God's sight, every secret lying bare before the One to whom we give account. The witnesses note the not manifest: "neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight" — Gill notes nothing is hidden from the omniscient Lord; the not manifest; the no creature; the hidden; the omniscient; the seen. The expositors observe the all creatures: every creature, rational or irrational, is known to him; the all creatures; the rational; the irrational; the known; the upheld. The witnesses press the naked and opened: "all things are naked and opened" — every thing lies bare, exposed; the naked and opened; the bare; the exposed; the laid open; the flayed. The expositors draw the lesson on the his eyes: "unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do" — before the One to whom we must give account; the his eyes; the with whom we have to do; the give account; the the Judge; the bare. The witnesses observe the sees every secret: the Judge sees every secret of the heart; the sees every secret; the the heart; the the Judge; the bare; the seen. The expositors note the no hiding: there is no hiding from him; the no hiding; the from him; the the warning; the solemn; the bare. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight, the all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do, and the all-seeing gaze. The verse thus records the all-seeing knowledge before which nothing is hidden — teaching that nothing in creation is hidden from God's sight, every secret lying bare before the One to whom we give account.
14:
"Seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession." The witnesses note the great High Priest who has passed into the heavens. Gill notes Christ is called a "great" high priest because of the dignity of his person, the virtue of his sacrifice, and the place where he now officiates — heaven itself, in distinction from the legal high priests. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, since we have a great high priest passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, we should hold fast our profession; the great High Priest in heaven as the ground of steadfast profession — that Paul turns from warning to encouragement, drawing comfort from Christ's priesthood: "we have a great high priest" — not a mere earthly priest like Aaron, but a "great" one, surpassing all, "that is passed into the heavens" (gone up through the heavens into the very presence of God, as the high priest into the holy place), and he is "Jesus the Son of God" (truly man and truly God); and on this firm ground Paul builds the exhortation: "let us hold fast our profession" — since we have so great and exalted a High Priest pleading for us in heaven, let us cling firmly to the faith we profess, not letting it go; let us hold fast our profession. The witnesses note we have a great high priest, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God. The witnesses note let us hold fast our profession.

The witnesses note the great High Priest in heaven — Jesus the Son of God, passed into the heavens — the ground of holding fast our profession; so great a High Priest pleading for us.

So the witnesses make the verse the great High Priest as the ground of steadfast profession. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since we have so great a High Priest in heaven, we should hold fast the faith we profess. The witnesses note the great high priest: "we have a great high priest" — Gill notes Christ a "great" priest, surpassing all; the great high priest; the great; the surpassing; the not Aaron; the dignity. The expositors observe the passed into heaven: "that is passed into the heavens" — gone into God's very presence; the passed into heaven; the God's presence; the the holy place; the gone up; the heaven. The witnesses press the Son of God: "Jesus the Son of God" — truly man and truly God; the Son of God; the truly man; the truly God; the Jesus; the divine. The expositors draw the lesson on the hold fast: "let us hold fast our profession" — cling firmly to the faith professed; the hold fast; the cling firmly; the the faith; the professed; the not let go. The witnesses observe the pleading for us: so great a High Priest pleading for us in heaven; the pleading for us; the in heaven; the so great; the the High Priest; the intercedes. The expositors note the firm ground: this is the firm ground of the exhortation; the firm ground; the the exhortation; the the ground; the build; the firm. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens Jesus the Son of God, the let us hold fast our profession, and the High Priest in heaven. The verse thus records the great High Priest as the ground of steadfast profession — teaching that, since we have so great a High Priest in heaven, we should hold fast the faith we profess.
15:
"For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin." The witnesses note the sympathy of the High Priest who was tempted yet sinless. Barnes notes our High Priest is not cold and unfeeling; he is one who is abundantly qualified to sympathize with us in our afflictions, having himself experienced our trials. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we have not a high priest who cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted as we are, yet without sin; the tender sympathy of a High Priest who shared our trials but not our sin — that Paul commends the great High Priest by his tender fellow-feeling: "we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities" (not a cold, distant priest unable to sympathize), but one who "was in all points tempted like as we are" — who in his earthly life met the whole range of human trial, temptation, and suffering, and so can feel for the tempted — "yet without sin"; the witnesses note this crucial qualification — Christ was tempted in every point, truly and really, yet he never yielded, never sinned, his temptations leaving him spotless — so that he is at once perfectly able to sympathize and perfectly holy; in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin. The witnesses note we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities. The witnesses note was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin.

The witnesses note the High Priest's tender sympathy — tempted in all points as we are, yet without sin; able to feel for the tempted, yet himself spotless and holy.

So the witnesses make the verse the sympathy of the sinless tempted High Priest. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ shared the whole range of human trial, so he can sympathize, yet without sin, so he is perfectly holy. The witnesses note the touched: "not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities" — Barnes notes not cold and unfeeling; the touched; the feeling; the our infirmities; the not cold; the sympathize. The expositors observe the qualified: abundantly qualified to sympathize, having experienced our trials; the qualified; the sympathize; the experienced; the our trials; the feel. The witnesses press the tempted in all points: "was in all points tempted like as we are" — met the whole range of human trial; the tempted in all points; the the whole range; the trial; the temptation; the suffering. The expositors draw the lesson on the can feel: so he can feel for the tempted; the can feel; the for the tempted; the sympathize; the fellow-feeling; the tender. The witnesses observe the without sin: "yet without sin" — he never yielded, never sinned; the without sin; the never yielded; the never sinned; the spotless; the holy. The expositors note the able and holy: at once able to sympathize and perfectly holy; the able and holy; the sympathize; the holy; the spotless; the both. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, the was in all points tempted like as we are yet without sin, and the sympathy of the sinless. The verse thus records the sympathy of the sinless tempted High Priest — teaching that Christ shared the whole range of human trial, so he can sympathize, yet without sin, so he is perfectly holy.
16:
"Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need." The witnesses note the invitation to come boldly to the throne of grace. Gill notes coming to Christ, who is the high priest spoken of and was typified by the mercy seat, is very proper; saints come to him for pardon and cleansing, and for the supply of every need. The witnesses note Paul inviting us to come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need; the bold access to the throne of grace through the sympathizing High Priest — that Paul draws the sweetest practical conclusion from Christ's priesthood: "let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace" — since we have such a High Priest, sympathizing and sinless, pleading above, we may draw near to God with confidence and freedom, not with slavish dread, to the very throne that, through Christ, is a "throne of grace" — and that with a sure expectation: "that we may obtain mercy" (the pardon of our sins) "and find grace to help in time of need" (timely, seasonable grace for every hour of trial); so that the believer, through his great High Priest, has free and confident access to all the help of God; let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace. The witnesses note let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace. The witnesses note that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need.

The witnesses note the bold access to the throne of grace through the sympathizing High Priest — coming with confidence to obtain mercy and find timely grace in every hour of need.

So the witnesses make the verse the bold access to the throne of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that, through the sympathizing High Priest, the believer may come confidently to God for mercy and timely grace. The witnesses note the come boldly: "let us therefore come boldly" — with confidence and freedom, not slavish dread; the come boldly; the confidence; the freedom; the not dread; the draw near. The expositors observe the throne of grace: "unto the throne of grace" — the throne that, through Christ, is one of grace; the throne of grace; the through Christ; the grace; the the mercy seat; the the throne. The witnesses press the obtain mercy: "that we may obtain mercy" — the pardon of our sins; the obtain mercy; the pardon; the our sins; the forgiveness; the mercy. The expositors draw the lesson on the grace to help: "and find grace to help in time of need" — timely, seasonable grace; the grace to help; the timely; the seasonable; the in need; the every hour. The witnesses observe the through the High Priest: this access is through the sympathizing High Priest; the through the High Priest; the sympathizing; the pleading; the Christ; the access. The expositors note the free access: the believer has free and confident access to God's help; the free access; the confident; the God's help; the the believer; the draw near. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, the that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need, and the bold access. The verse thus records the bold access to the throne of grace — teaching that, through the sympathizing High Priest, the believer may come confidently to God for mercy and timely grace.
